experiments.title: How testing whether an urgency-driven subject line or a specific biblical reference leads to higher opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a specific and intriguing biblical reference (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) instead of a more generic faith-based phrase (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because specificity and curiosity drive engagement, making the subject line feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/08/2025
Luther Seminary creates ebooks and digital resources to help their audience deepen their faith and biblical knowledge. Their latest ebook explores the book of Exodus, and they aimed to maximize engagement by optimizing the subject line of their promotional email to their housefile audience.
To determine what type of subject line would drive more opens and clicks, we conducted a series of A/B tests. In a prior test, we compared exclusivity-based language to a curiosity-driven subject line, finding that a curiosity-driving subject line increased opens by 9.4% with 100% statistical confidence.
For this next test, we wanted to know whether an urgency-based subject line (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) or a subject line that was more specific and intriguing (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) would drive higher engagement. We hypothesized that the more specific and curiosity-driven subject line would increase opens by making the content feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test produced unexpected but valuable insights about the relationship between subject line messaging and user engagement.

While the Control subject line ("Don't miss this opportunity to deepen your faith") generated more opens, the Treatment subject line ("Discover the richness of Exodus") resulted in fewer opens (a 3.8% decrease) but led to a 24.5% increase in email clicks, with a 98.4% statistical level of confidence.

This suggests that while urgency-driven language in the Control created a stronger initial impulse to open the email, the specificity of the Treatment subject line attracted a more engaged audience—those who opened were more likely to click through to the content.

Psychologically, this aligns with two key principles:
FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) and urgency – The Control subject line may have driven more opens by making the email feel time-sensitive and broadly relevant.
 	Relevance and commitment – The Treatment subject line set clearer expectations about the content, likely attracting recipients who were already interested in the topic, leading to higher engagement post-open.

experiments.title: How comparing a curiosity-driven subject line to an exclusivity-based subject line to determine impact on opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a vague, curiosity-driven subject line (“Here’s your first look”) instead of an exclusivity-based subject line (“Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because the curiosity gap encourages recipients to open the email to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 01/17/2025
Luther Seminary develops useful ebooks and other digital resources that help their audience deepen their faith and knowledge of the Bible. They recently created a new ebook about the book of Exodus and wanted to send it to their housefile email audience.
We wanted to know what kind of subject line would drive more opens and clicks for this content offer email, so we decided to run an A/B test.
The Control subject line used exclusivity messaging: “Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide.” The Treatment subject line was shorter and designed to spark curiosity: “Here’s your first look.”
We hypothesized that the curiosity-driven subject line would generate more opens by enticing recipients to learn more.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment confirmed that a curiosity-driven subject line can significantly increase email opens. The Treatment subject line ("Here's your first look") outperformed the Control ("Be the first to access our new Exodus Study Guide") by increasing opens 9.4%, with a 100% statistical level of confidence.

The success of the curiosity-based subject line aligns with the Curiosity Gap Theory, which suggests that people feel compelled to seek out missing information when presented with an incomplete or intriguing statement. Unlike the Control, which relied on exclusivity messaging, the Treatment sparked interest without revealing too much—prompting recipients to open the email to learn more.

However, while opens increased, click rates remained the same (~2.2% for both versions). This suggests that while curiosity successfully drove more people to open the email, it did not necessarily increase engagement with the content inside. A possible explanation is that the Treatment subject line created intrigue but did not set clear expectations about the offer, leading some recipients to open the email without a strong intention to engage further.

To build on this learning, future tests should explore:
Refining curiosity-driven subject lines – Testing variations that maintain intrigue while hinting more explicitly at the content's value.
 	Combining curiosity with faith-based language – Testing curiosity-driven phrasing with biblical references to see if it enhances both opens and clicks. Update: We ran a follow-up experiment to test curiosity-based language against a subject line featuring a specific biblical reference to see which approach is more effective at engaging faith-driven audiences.
 	Aligning email content with subject line expectations – Ensuring that the email itself delivers on the curiosity generated in the inbox, potentially increasing clicks.

experiments.title: How a less emotive subject line impacts the open rate of a sale email

rows.name: The true meaning of NIO Summit

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more direct subject line for email subscribers will achieve a higher open rate  because it’s clear what information they will find in the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/02/2024
Prior to our NIO Summit 2024 flash sale, we sent out an email to give our subscribers a heads-up about the upcoming sale in case they needed to get budget approval.
In an attempt to get higher visibility on the sale, we tested the subject line to see if a more direct subject line would increase email opens.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a more direct subject line for this type of NIO Summit email can significantly increase open rates, as demonstrated by the 21.1% increase in opens with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
This indicates that subscribers are more likely to open an email when they have a clear understanding of the information they will find inside.
Furthermore, the treatment email also resulted in a 678% increase in Clicks with a 100% level of confidence and a 386% increase in Clicks to Opens with a 95% level of confidence.
This shows that not only did the more direct subject line improve open rates, but it also drove more engagement and actions downstream from subscribers.
The treatment subject line also creates a more intense sense of urgency while also not giving everything away. It creates a sense of mystery — "What's the announcement?"
So, moving on from this experiment, we will continue testing ways to create more direct and urgent subject lines that clearly articulate the message in the email without giving it all away.

experiments.title: How does “opening a loop” in the subject line affect open rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an ‘open loop’ subject line will increase open rates by piquing people’s curiosity and creating a desire to ‘close the loop’ by finding out more about the update to which it alluded.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/20/2024
Since the primary job of a subject line is to win the open, we wanted to know if opening a loop of curiosity could improve open rates by giving less away. The control in this experiment directly stated what was inside the email, while the treatment merely mentioned an update.
The idea was that by piquing someone’s curiosity, or ‘opening a loop,’ the human desire to close that loop by finding out more about the update would prompt more people to open. Traffic was split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using an 'open loop' subject line produced a statistically valid 18.3% increase in open rates. By teasing an update without giving away specific information, people are more likely to be intrigued and feel the need to open the email to discover what the update is. This tactic taps into the human desire to close open loops and can be a powerful tool for increasing engagement.
The CTR of the treatment was lower (18.8%), but the sample size was insufficient to validate that outcome, so we will reserve drawing conclusions in that regard. In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of curiosity-driven subject lines and test against other concepts. We will also continue monitoring the impact on click-through rates. We should also consider if the length of the subject line (the treatment being shorter) played a part in this result.
Finally, there is probably a more effective way to run this test while mitigating the risk of diminished click-through rates by teasing the subject matter of the email without giving the content away, I.e., "This will boost donations" etc.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of a subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the subject line for email respondents will achieve an increase in open rate because the appeal extends to a larger audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The Navigators were sending out a cultivation email as the initial email for their Sustainers campaign. The control email used the subject line “What group is on your heart today?” The team hypothesized that this subject line assumed that people understood what the organization meant by “group”, and that the reader did in fact have a group on their heart.
They proposed a treatment, “Who are you praying for today?” that would increase the clarity and appeal to a larger group of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From this experiment, we have learned that a more clear and relatable subject line in an email can lead to an increase in open rate and ultimately, in this case, a higher rate of donations.

The key learning from this experiment is that using language that appeals to a wider audience and is easily understood can be more effective in engaging recipients and motivating them to take action. The treatment subject line, "Who are you praying for?" was able to resonate with a larger group of people compared to the control subject line, "What group is on your heart today?". This likely contributed to the increased open rate and subsequent increase in donations.

In future experimentation, we should prioritize using clear and relatable language in our subject lines to capture the attention of recipients and encourage them to open the email. Additionally, we should continue to test different variations of subject lines to optimize our messaging and maximize engagement and conversion rates.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of crafting subject lines that are clear, relatable, and appealing to a wide audience. By continuing to test and refine our messaging strategies, we can optimize our email campaigns and drive greater impact for our nonprofit organization.

rows.name: Looking out for your child’s education
A free educational program for kids

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to a subject line for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because recipients will gain a strong initial idea of what the message is about and what it might offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 06/08/2023
We want to test three different subject lines for ACI’s Little Patriots Pledge email to see which generates the most clicks. The purpose of the email is to send recipients to the pledge landing page where they would pledge to use and expand the Little Patriots curriculum.
The control subject line broadly mentions education
Treatment 1 subject line mentions a specific type of education
Treatment 2 subject line mentions an actual product – a “free educational program”
Let’s see which approach towards clarity in a subject line generates the most email clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Clarity proved to be the key to this subject line test. Each treatment produced a valid increase in email clicks. Treatment 1 produced a 145.8% increase in clicks while treatment 2 produced a 158.7% increase.

Our audience responded well to clarity. They appreciated knowing more information as they were considering opening the email, and surely the "free" callout in treatment two resonated strongly with them.

Interestingly, when looking at open rate, treatment 2 resulted in a decrease while treatment 1 resulted in a 2.6% increase with a 98% level of confidence. So treatment 2 produced a significant increase in opens and clicks.

Moving forward, we should implement more subject lines that focus on clarity, giving our audience up front transparency and value.

experiments.title: How opening a loop of curiosity via the subject line affects open rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a less direct subject line will increase open rates by creating an open loop of curiosity that recipients will want to close by opening the emails to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2022
During a year-end campaign when we know potential donors will be receiving a high volume of emails soliciting donations, we wanted to know whether or not using less direct subject lines would improve open rates by opening a loop of curiosity that the recipient would want to close by opening the email.
Recipients of this campaign were segmented between previous donors and non-donors and roughly half of both segments were sent emails with subject lines that spoke directly to the contents of the email while the other half received emails that opened a loop of curiosity with less direct subject lines … but which (importantly) would still clearly relate to the subject matter of the email once the recipient opened. This test was run across three appeal emails in an effort to ensure the results were not isolated to a single send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the results of this experiment a number of interesting observations came to light:
The curiosity-building subject lines did indeed increase open rates by 2.6% (99.9% LoC)However, the curiosity-building subject lines also decreased click-through rates by -8.5% (97.4% LoC)
But because these sends were all segmented between previous donors and non-donors, drilling down into the data may help paint a more complete picture of our results, for instance:
Among non-donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 2.4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)But they also produced a 16.6% decrease in click-through rates  (100% LoC)
Among previous donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)While also producing a 33.8% increase in click-through rates (99.9% LoC)
And so we see that not all recipients behave the same, further emphasizing the importance of tailoring communications to the audience that you are targeting, and doing away with the assumption that all audiences behave the same.
Non-donors may be less familiar with (or less bought into) the value American Cornerstone Institute provides—hence their status as non-donors—while previous donors, who better understand (or better align) with the value received from ACI were hooked by the curiosity-building subject line and continued their momentum through the appeal contained inside the email, ultimately clicking through to the donation page at a significantly higher rate.
Based on these results, we will want to send emails with direct subject lines to the non-donor segment while sending curiosity-building emails to the previous donor segments as the results from this experiment have clearly indicated each segment's preference.

experiments.title: How a subject line invoking curiosity impacted click rates on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enhancing curiosity in a subject line for housefile email subscribers will achieve an increase in email engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 11/10/2022
When crafting an email to the housefile announcing the goal of 100,000 survey submissions had been reached, we wanted to test the subject line to get a learning about our housefile audience and what they respond to in order to help build future subject lines to use in an upcoming campaign. Would they be more motivated to open and take the intended desired action from an email with a subject line that did not include curiosity and rather gave clarity, revealing the contents of the message? Or would they be more compelled to open and click a message that related to an issue they care about and promises a reveal. In this case, the control was “100,000 … and counting” referring to the 100K survey submission goal and the treatment subject line was “This is great news”
experiments.keyLearnings: We measured comparative open rates for each version of the email and saw a 4.3% lift in opens on the treatment version. However, we know that open rate is no longer a reliable metric due to inflated open rates being counted by audience members who are using Apple Mail. So we wanted to dig a little deeper to investigate the overall results on email engagement. We also measured the number of clicks on the Call to Action in the body copy for each version. Although the emails were identical aside from the subject line, we saw a 61.4% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence on the treatment. We believe this is due to the positive momentum the subject line carries from "This is great news" into reminding people it wasn't too late to submit their survey. If the reader was curious to see what the great news was, they had an increased chance of seeing the call to action inviting them to complete the survey as well. And since the message leaned into aligning with the subscriber's values and highlighted social proof, they could now have a compelling reason to say "yes" to the ask and be a part of the continued great news.

experiments.title: How will a direct request in a subject line affect click rates?

rows.name: A surprise for Dr. Carson's birthday
Will you help me celebrate Dr. Carson's birthday?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a direct request in a subject line will improve click-through rates for a housefile send when compared to a less direct subject line.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2022 - 09/22/2022
In a recent housefile email send promoting the opportunity for subscribers to sign a birthday card for Dr. Ben Carson, we wanted to test how a direct request subject line that puts the call to action front and center affects open rates. We tested this against a subject line that was less direct in mentioning a “birthday surprise,” and made no other changes to the email. We sent each subject line to 50% of the house file to assess the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Including a direct request in the subject line resulted in a 4.5% lift in click rates with a 96.6% level of confidence. We believe this lift occurred because the direct request better aligned recipient expectations as to what the email is all about.

The direct request version of the subject line makes it clear that the recipient is being asked to help celebrate Dr. Ben Carson's birthday in some way while the less direct subject line merely mentions a "surprise," without alluding to the fact that it in some way involves the recipient.

As a result, the recipients who received the direct request subject line were more primed to "help" and thus more likely to click through on a call to action that matches this expectation. Recipients of the less direct subject line may have thought they were simply being informed of a surprise, making them less primed to participate in some way.

Humans crave consistency and this test suggests that by setting an early expectation and keeping that expectation consistent through to the CTA, you are more likely to see engagement with the offer being promoted.

experiments.title: How subject line urgency impacted email performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 01/13/2017
As the year end deadline approached for Illinois Policy Institute’s year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to find ways to communicate that urgency to the subscribers. For the second-to-last email send, sent out on December 30th, they decided to run a subject line test to determine which would have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement.
Both of the subject lines communicated urgency. The primary difference between the two was that one, time is running out, is more direct in its approach and is a more common approach taken by many organizations in their fundraising and marketing-style emails. The alternative, tick tock, was a bit less common and did not give away the purpose of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more direct and “sale-y” subject line of time is running out decreased the open rate by 8%. Additionally, this lower open rate led to an incrementally lower click through rate with a 33% decrease (with 94.6% statistical validity). Our hypothesis is that with the time is running out subject line, the subscribers immediately felt marketed to since it is not a subject line most people would use in their day to day communications. As a result, they were less likely to engage.

This is a great example of the concept that people give to people. As fundraisers, we want to communicate urgency but we need to do it in such a way that it still feels genuine.

experiments.keyLearnings:
With almost a 100% LoC (99.9%) the unique, mystery subject line led to an increase in opens which more than anything underscores how changing up the type of subject lines used can help keep people engaged. In this case, the [square brackets] may have helped it stand out but our theory is the more humourous and unique element of the subject line helped lead to more opens.

Interestingly, with a true 100% LoC (100%) we saw a 93.3% increase in click rate and a 63.8% increase in click to open rate (99.9% LoC) with the unique, mysterious subject line. This underscores both the relationship between the open rate and click rate and how the subject line can influence a reader's motivation before they even get into the body/content of the email itself.

experiments.title: How the power of not “breaking a streak” affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a streak of giving for key multiyear donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/22/2022
CaringBridge was running an appeal to celebrate their 25th anniversary as a company. They had read research about how people hate breaking streaks and strive for continuity, and wanted to test the inclusion of this language in their appeals with a group of consistent donors.
They took a “control” email from the campaign and kept it largely the same, but changed the subject line from “There’s Still Time to Help Mark the 25th Birthday of CaringBridge, [FirstName]” to “Don’t Break the Streak, [FirstName]!”. Then, in the copy they highlighted the fact that the donors had previously given to CaringBridge and asked them to continue their streak of generosity.
Finally, the email thanked them for their continued, generous giving.
They split the list of donors in half to see how they responded to the two different messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment group that received the "continuity" messaging showed a 65.4% increase in conversion rate, bolstered by an 81% increase in clickthrough rate.
This shows evidence of "commitment bias", where people have a tendency to remain committed to our past behaviors, particularly those exhibited publicly, even if they do not have desirable outcomes.
This is seen in the for-profit space in concepts like Duolingo streaks or Snapchat streaks, but also is applicable to charitable giving through concepts like this.
It should be noted that this likely has limited application as there is a strong possibility for diminishing returns if overused—but the results can be powerful!

experiments.title: How writing a direct subject line increased click rates

rows.name: An Email From a Ukrainian Bomb Shelter
Ukrainian students need your help today

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a direct subject line for email recipients will achieve higher click rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2022 - 04/06/2022
Curiosity is often used in subject lines to improve open rates. Prevailing wisdom states that when an email recipient’s interest is piqued by an unexpected subject line, they almost can’t help but open.
But we wanted to take a closer look at how such an approach would affect click rates—which is, after all, the ultimate goal of email marketing. Specifically, we wanted to know if using curiosity in the subject line might set a false expectation for the reader and thus have a negative impact on click rates. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test, the treatment that used a more direct subject line resulted in a 5.9% lift in click rate with a 23.1% level of confidence. And while the low level of confidence prevents us from validating the results, we can still draw helpful conclusions from this experiment.
It is interesting to note that  the direct subject line treatment lifted click rates while producing a 6.2% decrease in open rates. This can likely be attributed to two factors:
The subject line, "An email from a Ukrainian Bomb shelter" opens a loop in the recipient's mind, prompting them to open the email to learn more, HOWEVER ...While curiosity improved open rates, it may also have set a false expectation (or failed to set any expectation) in the minds of recipients about the content of the email itself ... whereas the more direct subject line, "Ukrainian students need your help today," while less enticing, sets a much clearer expectation as to what the recipient can expect to find when they open—an appeal to help Ukrainian students.
It is helpful to remember that while open rates are nice (and curiosity subject lines are admittedly fun to write), the ultimate goal of any email that contains a call to action / link is to get the recipient to click through.
When asking email recipients to take a specific action in the body of the email, organizations may be able to improve their click rates by setting a clear expectation in the subject line and thus creating a more consistent experience (and less friction) for potential donors.

Taking this into consideration, moving forward we will continue to use personalization and mystery in subject lines to maintain and improve overall email engagement. We want the monthly newsletter recipients to not just open the email but to also engage with it and receive value from the contents included. Seeing the small bump in open rate is not nearly as valuable when we simultaneously saw such a decline on the treatment clickthrough rate. Looking ahead, we'd rather have the file be more deeply engaged in a way that they are taking that next step to demonstrate they are interested, connected and invested than to have a 1.3% improvement on the open rate for future newsletters. This is why it's important to fully measure our results.

“Desired effect” language has proven powerful before, but in this case, clarity trumped persuasion.

experiments.title: Which is more effective in a subject line: “Mystery + Value” or “Mystery + Hyper-Personalization”

rows.name: A new fundraising webinar you’ll want to mark your calendar for
Matt, I’ve been working on this for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of subject line elements will lead to greater opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2020 - 03/18/2020
In this experiment, I was using NextAfter’s 5-step subject line creation tool to craft a subject line for a new webinar. I ended up with 2 different, equally viable subject lines. The first emphasized “utility” or “value” while not giving away the exact topic of the webinar. The second was equally “mysterious” while laying heavily into personalization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The heavily personalized version led to a 25.5% increase in opens at a 99.9% level of confidence. But interestingly enough, there was no statistically significant change in the number of people who signed up for the webinar.
This is yet another example of how the subject line plays a crucial role in setting up how someone will receive and perceive the rest of the email. In this case, it's likely that people opened the treatment because of the additional personalization and mystery, but ultimately weren't inclined to join a webinar. While the opens generated by the control were already primed to engage with a webinar - leading to a higher conversion rate from those that did open the email.
In a case like this where the net signups are a wash - I will always err on the side of the email with greater opens. At the most basic level, getting more opens can lead to better deliverability, and lead to stronger relationships with the folks on our email file.

experiments.keyLearnings: By sending a plain text email, we were able to increase email engagement by 19%. Not only that, but we also saw an increase in opens - even though the subject lines were identical. This tells us that we had increased deliverability and inboxing. People appreciate hearing from Arnie - as a person. The tone and copy of the email was believable that he actually sent the email to the file. By stripping the email of the marketing elements (images, template, etc) we were able to reinforce this sentiment as well.

experiments.title: How a personalizing the “envelope” of an email affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalizing the “envelope” of an email affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2019
Compassion International invited young college graduates to join their Compassion Go internship program through an email offer. The email came from “Compassion International” as the sender, and they wanted to know if personalizing the “envelope” on the email could increase open rate. They changed the sender to the name of an intern, and changed the subject line from a very sales-heavy subject line to “Quick question”. Then, they ran a test to see which one produced a higher open rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment email had a massive 329% increase in open rate. However, this "personal style" wasn't just applied to the subject line and sender—the entire email was inviting, and gave the recipient the opportunity to begin a conversation with a person, rather than receive marketing from an organization. Compassion saw that the treatment email, in addition to the lift in open rate, received 7x more submissions to the program than the control, and facilitated a tremendous amount of inbound responses beyond that, proving that personalization helps transcend "marketing".

experiments.title: How we can validate the impact in changing the sender
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a straight-forward subject line (Latest attack on freed) get better engagement than one that is more sensational (Political Correctness run amuck)?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/08/2019
Alliance Defending Freedom had by running a 5 month long cultivation experiment where half of their email file received weekly cultivation sends from an ADF staff member (Sarah.) Due to Sarah going on leave due to maternity, we ended up having to change the sender. In the week prior, Maureen took over for Sarah after being introduced the week before. In this first send, we saw the lowest open rate we had seen with this entire experiment.
This lower open rate was cause for concern since the entire purpose of these emails is to get engagement with the file. We decided to run a subject line experiment to see if we can find a subject that would get the level of engagement we were use to seeing with these emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment proved two things for us:
Maureen is able to get the same level of engagement as we are use to seeing with Sarah as the sender.The more straight-forward subject line received an 11.8% increase in email engagement.

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will achieve a higher open rate: one with personalization, a call to action, and a synonym for the word “free”? Or one without personalization, phrased as a question, and using the word “free”?

Ended On: 02/07/2019
Hillsdale College recently sent out an email that offered the opportunity for the recipient to receive a pocket-sized Constitution booklet for absolutely no cost. The initial email was opened and clicked through by many, but a follow-up email was necessary to ensure as many people as possible had the chance to get their free pocket Constitution. Two distinct subject lines were crafted for this follow-up email, and the results regarding the effectiveness of these subject lines were recorded.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As predicted, the treatment subject line, was effective in achieving a higher open rate than the control subject line. In fact, the open rate for the treatment was 2.03% higher than the control. The treatment contained personalization, a synonym for the word “free,” and a call to action instead of a question. The combination of all of these resulted in a higher likelihood of the receiver opening the email.
The main purpose of the experiment was to measure the difference in open rates between the two subject lines, and those results were conclusive. However, the control subject line did achieve a higher click-through rate than the treatment. Looking into why this was the case could be the basis for future research.

experiments.title: How subject line urgency impacted email performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 01/13/2017
As the year end deadline approached for Illinois Policy Institute’s year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to find ways to communicate that urgency to the subscribers. For the second-to-last email send, sent out on December 30th, they decided to run a subject line test to determine which would have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement.
Both of the subject lines communicated urgency. The primary difference between the two was that one, time is running out, is more direct in its approach and is a more common approach taken by many organizations in their fundraising and marketing-style emails. The alternative, tick tock, was a bit less common and did not give away the purpose of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more direct and “sale-y” subject line of time is running out decreased the open rate by 8%. Additionally, this lower open rate led to an incrementally lower click through rate with a 33% decrease (with 94.6% statistical validity). Our hypothesis is that with the time is running out subject line, the subscribers immediately felt marketed to since it is not a subject line most people would use in their day to day communications. As a result, they were less likely to engage.
This is a great example of the concept that people give to people. As fundraisers, we want to communicate urgency but we need to do it in such a way that it still feels genuine.

experiments.title: How urgency in the subject line impacts open and click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding urgency to the subject line increase the percentage of people the open or click on the message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2017 - 12/30/2016
World Concern sent a series of 7 fundraising emails as part of their year end campaign. They wanted to ensure each email was getting the maximum impact so they set up different treatments for each email.
For the second email in the series, we wanted to discover if adding urgency to the subject line would impact the percentage of people that either opened or clicked on the message. We decided to test a more vague subject line against one that conveyed the urgency of the December 31st deadline since that was the topic of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the end, the more urgent subject line of “Don’t wait another minute” decreased the open rate of the email by 27.5%. However, we also wanted to monitor how it impacted the click through rate since the ultimate goal of this email was to get people to the donation page. When analyzed, we discovered there was not a significant difference between the percentage of people clicking on a link.
As a rule of thumb, it is a good trade off to get less opens if it means more people clicking on the link but with there being no discernible difference between click rates, we would rather have more people reading the email so they are exposed to the message when emails later in the series are sent.

We also tested a radical redesign of the email and a completely new copy approach, which also produced a lift in clickthrough rate. This is a reminder of a key principle from the Fundraiser’s Creed: people give to people, not organizations or marketing machines.

experiments.title: Gmail’s handling of full-format vs. low-format emails
experiments.researchQuestion: Does Gmail treat low-format emails differently than full-format emails?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 08/10/2017
We had already been testing a low-format version of our emails (no tables, no images, limited CSS, visible links) and seeing that they performed several percentage points better in open rate than the fully formatted versions. We suspected that one or more email clients was treating low-format emails differently than full-format emails, and our first suspect was Google due to its tabbed inbox system.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It turns out that, with no changes to email address, subject line or preheader text, Gmail directs low-format emails into the Primary inbox and full-format emails into the Promotions tab. With the email in the Primary inbox, open rates increase by 50-60%. This has held true for nearly a dozen sends over two months.
This indicates that a vast majority of Gmail users don’t know about the Promotions tab or forget to look there. We’ve also determined that most Gmail mobile apps make the Promotions area hard to find, although Google now includes Promotions notifications in the Primary inbox.
As a further note, no other email client shows a statistically significant improvement from a low-format version, and AT&T properties show a negative impact.

experiments.title: How assumption of desire for an offer affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will assuming the recipient wants an offer affect open rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2019
Hillsdale College was sending complimentary pocket Constitutions to their constituents and donors. They knew they had a list of people who had not received a free pocket Constitution, but they weren’t sure if the people wanted a pocket Constitution. They decided to test two different approaches in the subject line. The first approach asked a question—”Do you want a free pocket Constitution?” The second assumed that they did want it, and offered them to them: “Get your complimentary pocket Constitution”. They split the file and sent a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that assumed the recipient wanted the offer produced a 30% lift in open rate. There are two possible reasons for this. First, the question format allowed the recipient to decide whether or not to open the email before they even opened the email. If they didn't want it, many people may have just archived or deleted the email. Second, the two subject lines also used two different words to describe the offer: "free" and "complimentary". This may have had an impact, as "free" implies that the offer is widely available and of limited value, while "complimentary" implies that the offer is of some value but is given free of charge due to a relationship. This element should be specifically targeted to determine impact in a future test.

experiments.title: How the fear of loss of membership benefits increased donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adjusting the motivation of a message to focus on the “fear of loss” increase donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2019 - 02/12/2019
Within the 2019 Heritage Foundation Kickoff Campaign, most of the control messaging delivered was highly transaction — largely focusing upon driving members online to renew their membership for the year. On the final day of the campaign, we wanted to test adjusting motivation for the file that had not yet given by treating the email with what they would lose if they didn’t renew their membership benefits, as opposed to the control message (which was tailoring motivation towards “hope of gain”).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.2% level of confidence, we successfully validated this experiment for the treatment ("fear of loss" message) with a 21.7% lift in donor conversion rates.
Other metrics impacted are noted as follows (all values shown are relative to the control version's performance in the same performance metric category):
Email Open Rate: +2.0% (with a 87.2% LoC)Email Clickthrough Rate: -4.6% (with a 64.4% LoC)On-Page Conversion Rate: +8.5%Revenue/Visitor: +4.2%Average Gift: +6.0%
Further experimentation should be attempted, however these results are encouraging that "fear of loss" may be a better motivator for members to renew their membership, as opposed to the "hope of gain."
We theorize that the personal impact of the loss is potentially felt more than the collective gain that we together can create. The tangibility of the impact of renewing their gift is more poignant, and is felt more -- which appears to have driven the increase in motivation to go online and renew.
These messages were sent on the morning of the final day. They both included referencing that it was the final day (e.g. created a sense of urgency to take action today), and structurally they were the same (in terms of sender, subject line, template, etc).
We would advise using a "fear of loss" message at least on the final day of a campaign, but can it provide good lift earlier in the campaign without weakening the final day message, as well? Further experimentation is recommended on timing within the campaign timeline.

More importantly, when a subject line becomes personal or sounds like the sender is personally emailing the recipient, people are even more motivated to open it. The email starts to feel less like a marketing appeal and more like an email you would receive from someone who knows you. It is important to talk to donors and prospective donors like they are people – even as early on in the communication process as the subject line.

experiments.title: How “human” fundraising subject lines affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “humanized copy” increase open rate and increase motivation to click?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2016 - 04/19/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending their mid-month email, which was a highly transactional, premium-focused offer. The offer featured a book that was relevant to Mother’s Day, which was coming up in just a few weeks. The treatment subject line was appropriately transactional: “Order Today to Receive for Mother’s Day”. We had been running some experiments with “humanized” copy — that is, copy that a normal person would write, rather than copy that is explicitly “marketing”. We hypothesized that a more “human” subject line would increase open rate, but we also wanted to measure the effect on clickthrough rate as well, since the two do not operate independently.
At the same time as the subject line test, we were running an email copy and design test as well. The control (which was matched to the control subject line) incentivized the click, a strategy that has shown to attract more clicks, but often less motivated traffic. The treatment made a more direct ask, which often results in fewer clicks but more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment subject line actually delivered a statistically valid 7.5% increase in open rate. Which is good…if the experiment ended there. But the ultimate goal was gifts, which relies on a click. So we needed to measure the entire funnel.
Once we looked past the subject line, we saw that the treatment email (which made the direct ask) produced a 59% decrease in clickthrough rate, which resulted in a 43% drop in conversion. So, the increase in open rate didn’t really matter that much. It highlights an important learning: open rate is only the beginning of the relationship. Getting 7.5% more people to open the email is a win, but not if it doesn’t pay off when we look at our ultimate metric.
This shows us that “human” subject lines can work, but they may affect reader motivation differently. The control subject line, though it had fewer opens, resulted in many more people showing interest in the offer — most likely due to a combination of the urgency it produced and the design of the control email.

The treatment was personal, but in hindsight, the control may have been even more personal because Greg mentions his son’s name in the subject line. Readers were more interested in what Greg had to say or tell them about his son Jonathan than some self-proclaimed “…personal story” he had to share. The treatment didn’t provide enough clarity with what the personal story was about and fewer people opened as a result.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to a repositioned call to action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will highlighting the urgency of a deadline increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2015 - 12/09/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium. On the landing page, there was a callout box that indicated that time was running out to order the premium and receive it before Christmas.
Their team formed a hypothesis that if they moved this callout earlier in the donation funnel, that they would incentivize more readers of the email to click through to the page. They developed a treatment that added urgent copy that emphasized the impending deadline, and moved the call-to-action button after this copy.  They then ran an A/B split test to see the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tells us that highlighting urgency on the landing page isn’t enough — we must move it earlier in the decision process if we want to motivate and incentivize the maximum amount of prospects. Because email performance rates decline naturally at each step (from delivery to open rate to clickthrough rate), we can reach more prospects with this message as we move it further down the inverted funnel. However, that doesn’t mean that we should move it to the subject line — when improperly placed, urgency can deceive and disincentivize prospects.

This also gives a valuable lesson about call-to-action placement. In the first email, the button was placed before the premium, which meant that many prospects made the decision before they were fully informed and incentivized. By moving the button to the end of the email (and strengthening the incentive that comes before it), Harvest was able to nearly double clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How inviting an email recipient to participate affects email open rate

Timeframe: 12/06/2017 - 12/13/2017
Hillsdale College recently began their calendar year end campaign. This year they decided to focus more specifically on their independence and how it enables them to fulfill their mission–without accepting EVEN ONE PENNY of government funding! One of their appeal emails focused on their presence in Washington D.C. through the Kirby Center. Before sending, they wondered: Will recipients be more motivated to open an email that invites them to participate in an action (in this case, helping teach policymakers about the constitution). They created an A/B subject line test and monitored the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Hillsdale College found that the treatment version actually decreased email open rate by about 5%. Though the relative impact of this test was small, it provides a valuable learning for future email subject line creation. Though the invitation to ‘Help teach policymakers the Constitution’ feels like a noble thing to ask recipients, it’s clear that it halted some in their inbox. It’s likely that the use of the word  ‘help’ struck some as a plea. This word makes people feel like the impetus is on them to do something. This can produce unnecessary anxiety in the mind of the recipient before they even have a chance to read the email.
This is an important thing to remember for any organization that is running a calendar year end campaign, and it will continue to inform Hillsdale College’s approach to email communication.

They created a treatment version with the words ‘Fake News’ and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Hillsdale College found that the treatment version did increase email open rate by nearly 9%. It’s clear that the use of the term ‘Fake News’ attracted more attention, and therefore, increased motivation of the email recipients. This is important to remember when crafting subject lines during anytime of the year, but especially during a calendar year end campaign. Email recipients are flooded with emails during the last month of the year, and it’s easy to get lost in the shuffle. You can increase your email open rates by using terms that recipients immediately recognize and have an interest in, as long as they are directly related to the topic you are discussing in the email copy.

experiments.title: How a subject line’s perceived impact on the subscriber influences click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the greatest email engagement (opens and clicks)?

Timeframe: 03/07/2017 - 03/17/2017
In an effort to educate their subscriber base on the public’s sentiment toward the state’s current budget crisis, the Illinois Policy Institute sent an email with the results of an independent 3rd party poll. The results of the poll aligned with the message IPI had been promoting since the current session began. It also supported the budget plan the the organization had developed and was working with state legislators to pass.
The goal of the email was to educate and engage the subscriber file so we decided to test out two subject lines that highlighted the contents of the message: “tax hikes” and “overspending.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
After sending to half of the email file, we found that “overspending” decreased the overall click through rate by 23%. It also negatively impacted the open rate by 5%. It is our hypothesis that this behavior is due to how the words are perceived by the subscriber. A “tax hike” is something that would impact them directly while “overspending” is something that they may not be in favor of but does not appear have any direct impact on them.
This serves as an important reminder that all aspects of the messaging should focus on how the topic will impact the recipient (when possible). We need to address not only why a message is important but why it is important to the reader.

experiments.title: How subject line copy that “teases” affects open rate

They were promoting it by renting email lists, and wanted to determine which subject lines encouraged more recipients to open the email. They developed two subject lines based on hypotheses about the prospect. The first was very direct: “Study the Gospel of John with DTS”. The second teased the offer, and expanded from the acronym: “A special offer from Dallas Theological Seminary”.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that prospects are more likely to open an email when we tease the contents. So this suggests that teasing recipients of this email might increase open rate, which is the metric that affects every other following metric.

experiments.title: How subject line personalization affects open rate

experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the personalized subject line produced a 22.5% lift in open rate, which showed that including the recipient’s first name in the subject line increases open rate.

But the personalization wasn’t the only factor—the treatment subject line also obscured the actual payoff of the email by not disclosing the offer. This appeared to heighten interest in what the “reveal” would be, intensified by the personalization factor.

The combination of these two factors led to a significant increase in open rate, which helped inform future subject line copy. However, the “trick” of personalization also may come with a significant diminishing return if overused.

Timeframe: 10/05/2016 - 10/06/2016
Hillsdale College sent prospecting emails to a Townhall Media email file to generate signups for its free Pocket Constitution offer. Our hypothesis was that subject line could effect open rate, but that by clarifying the value proposition, the subject line could also drive a more motivated and qualified reader to the landing page, resulting in higher conversion rates.
The file was randomized into four segments, with each segment receiving the control or one of the three treatment versions. The subject line was the only variable changed between each test. All other email elements and landing pages were identical.
The subject lines used were:
+Control: Keep this in your pocket
+Treatment#1: Keep THIS in Your Pocket
+Treatment#2: Get Your Free Pocket Constitution Now
+Treatment#3: Free Pocket Constitution, courtesy of Hillsdale College
In this case, Samples count is the number of emails sent, and emails acquired is the total number of people who completed the signup process on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: Sometimes more ambiguous subject lines can tease a reader into opening an email. In this case, explicitly stating the value proposition at the front of the subject line drove higher clicks and higher conversions. This is the case when the value proposition is very direct and simple to understand (“Free Pocket Constitution”). People who opened  Treatments #2 and #3 are more qualified than those of the more ambiguous subject lines of Control and Treatment#1. Mentioning “Hillsdale College” serves to evoke credibility from a reputable and established institution that is well known among the target audience.

experiments.title: How does addressing someone directly and asking them a personal question affect click-through rate for a high urgency email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors will achieve a higher click-through rate because it keeps the user engaged and helps them feel more relatable to the situation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators launched their calendar year campaign with an email that started with a direct statement about how Christians face problems more now than ever before. We decided to test how opening an email with a personalized question to draw them in would affect click-through rate to the donation page.
In previous testing, we’ve seen a more personalized tone perform better because it allows the user to relate to the problem at hand and causes them to engage with the email longer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors can have a positive impact on click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the personalized question at the beginning of the email, saw a 20.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group with an 87.2% level of confidence.

Because of these results, we believe that opening an email with a personalized question rather than a direct statement will keep users engaged with the email causing them to be more likely to click the link to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of personalized tones and messaging to optimize click-through rates. Additionally, it would be important to analyze other metrics such as conversions and donations to understand the full impact of the personalized approach. By continuously testing and iterating, we can refine our email strategies to effectively engage potential donors and drive meaningful action.

experiments.title: How a mysterious subject line + conversational email copy impacted email clicks

rows.name: Version A - Subject Line: The taxes you pay

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a mysterious subject line coupled with conversational email copy for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because more people will open the email to find out the meaning of the mysterious subject line and the conversational appeal of the email will lead more people to click to find out more about the focus article of the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI is sending a monthly cultivation email to their housefile email list. In order to learn about our audience and what motivates them to open emails and click within emails, we’ve set up a subject line + body copy experiment.
1/4 of our list will receive
control subject line and control body copy
treatment subject line with control body copy
control subject line with treatment body copy
treatment subject line with treatment body copy
The treatment subject line plays into curiosity and intrigue, while the treatment body copy takes an especially conversational approach, sharing the perspective of the sender while also asking for the thoughts and feedback of the reader.
We want to see which combination generates the most clicks within the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment #2 (treatment subject line + control email copy) produced a nearly valid (89.9% level of confidence) 11.9% increase in email clicks. Treatments #1 (control SL and treatment email copy) and #3 (treatment SL and control email copy) produced negative results with invalid level of confidences.

Thus, treatment #2 was the big winner here. Our audience has responded well to mystery subject lines in the past, and we now have more evidence that this approach to subject line framing is effective. These type of subject lines creative intrigue and curiosity, prompting recipients to open the email to find out what's inside. The control subject line did not have the same curiosity, as it exposed the topic up front.

When we look specifically at open rates, treatment #2 produced a 49.3% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence; treatment #3 produced a 24.5% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence.

Ultimately, we should lead many more cultivation emails using mysterious subject lines.

As for the body copy element, perhaps the conversational approach consisted of too much fluff, as the control – less conversational – variant was a bit more to the point and brief, and resulted in higher click rates. We may want to be more considerate of our recipients' time and focus more on clarity and being direct when sending future cultivation emails.

While we didn’t have enough applications to validate off of conversions (3 for the control and 1 for the treatment), this decrease in clicks is a strong indicator that focusing on time constraints is not the right value proposition focus.

experiments.title: How an actionable and enticing subject line impacts clicks

three steps to deepen your relationships…

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which used a more actionable subject line, did not perform better than the control in terms of Clicks for All Traffic. In fact, it experienced a 2.5% decrease in clicks.

3. The statistical level of confidence for the Clicks for All Traffic results was only 22.9%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experiments, it is important to consider the following:

1. While creating more actionable subject lines may increase open rates, it may not necessarily lead to more clicks. It is important to strike a balance between creating curiosity and providing clear information in the subject line.

2. It is crucial to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making any conclusions or decisions based on the outcome of the experiment.

3. Further experimentation and testing may be needed to fully understand the impact of different subject line strategies on email performance.

experiments.title: How does increasing the relevancy of a subject line affect email opens

The Navigators sent a cultivation email as apart of their calendar year end campaign that offered a Christmas Reading Plan for free to their housefile. 2. But while the treatment showed a slight increase in opens, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not valid.

To improve future experimentation, testing different variations of subject lines and analyzing the impact on email engagement can provide further insights. experiments.title: How stating the “deadline” in a subject line for a high urgency campaign impacted open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving clarity to an email subject line amid a high urgency campaign for email recipients will achieve an increase in email open rate because recipients will know their exact window to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/13/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute is amid a high urgency fundraising campaign. The campaign deadline is tomorrow, and we want to test how making that clear in the subject line of today’s email impacts open rate.
The control variant (subject line A) is “Urgent reminder” while the treatment variant (subject line B) is “Deadline tomorrow”. We’ve split these variants to where 50% of our recipients will experience one subject line and 50% will experience the other.
We want to find out how our recipients respond to each subject line in terms of choosing to open the email. We expect “Deadline tomorrow” to produce the most opens because it clearly communicates the deadline to recipients and the fact that the current opportunity will expire after tomorrow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment variant, which had the subject line "Deadline tomorrow", did not perform better than the control variant, which had the subject line "Urgent reminder". The treatment variant saw a 4.8% decrease in Opens for All Traffic with a 70.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, we cannot conclude that giving clarity to the subject line in a high urgency fundraising campaign leads to an increase in email open rate.
It's also worth noting that the treatment subject line produced a 32.3% drop in click rate with an 82.2% level of confidence.
The "Deadline tomorrow" subject line may have been less effective because recipients didn't know what the deadline was for. Instead of opening the email to find out, they may have decided it wasn't worth their time to find out. The "Urgent reminder" SL on the other hand, tells the recipient that they've been told about the opportunity before and they may then feel more inclined to open the email see what they need to be "reminded" about.
From a psychological standpoint, there is also the possibility that the word "deadline" is a turn off to recipients. Humans often view deadlines in a negative context because they're used to hitting deadlines at work or with tasks in life. They may not take kindly to the mention of another deadline.
This angle is worth testing again for a future high urgency email to get more data behind it.

experiments.title: How a email subject line drives clicks

rows.name: 10 Core Beliefs for Standing Strong in a Shifting World
Brand-new book "What Every Christian Should Know"

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding clarity to the subject line for the housefile will achieve an increase in click conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2023 - 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth. In that effort, each month PTV shares a book or resource to equip Christians to share the truth of God.
The premium for January 2023 is a brand-new book by Dr. Robert Jeffress titled “What Every Christian Should Know”.
In an effort to drive premium purchases, we decided to test the subject lines in the initial kick-off email to see which resonated more with the individuals on the email list. For our treatment, we added clarity by announcing the release of the brand-new book, while the control stayed in alignment with the traditional approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this test, we saw 2 different results.
For the "10 core beliefs" subject, we saw a 5.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence in opens. However, we know that this is not a conversion rate that should be totally trusted due to various platforms "pre-opening" emails.
For the "Brand-new book" subject line, we saw a 10% increase in clicks with a level of confidence at 65.4%.
What we found interesting is that more people open the email with the subject line "10 core beliefs" however the higher click count occurred on the "Brand-new book" subject line. This leads us to believe that people are looking for information around the core beliefs but were not as interested in purchasing a book. Whereas the "brand-new book" subject line saw more clicks meaning that people knew what they were opening and were more interested in learning more about the book.
For this new book, we are going to continue testing copy and seeing which angle of this book resonates best in an effort to drive purchases.

experiments.title: How a problem-specific subject line impacted email engagement in a high urgency campaign

rows.name: Treatment - The Left is relentless. So are you.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding mystery to the subject line for the housefile in a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in the open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
During a high urgency campaign, most of the subject lines were in reference to membership and were relevant to the campaign. We wanted to find out if a problem-oriented subject line that held some mystery about the contents of the message would increase open and click rates for the email. We split the file 50/50 and send the control subject line “About your membership” vs. “The Left is relentless. So are you.” both from the same sender.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not validate the experiment since we only reached a 16% level of confidence that there was a .24% increase in the open rate on the treatment. Since there was no significant change observed, we will have to test this concept again on future emails or try other methods to improve the email engagement rate during a high urgency campaign with frequent emails. We want to find out if the housefile is more responsive to membership-specific messages concerning their status or if they are more intrigued by the problem we are facing and need their help to solve.

experiments.title: How specificity in a subject line impacts open rate

rows.name: You should read this with your family
You should read this to your kids

experiments.researchQuestion: Will specificity in the subject line increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 11/24/2020
Care Net was sending a cultivation email to their file and wanted to test the element of specificity in their subject line. The control was “You should read this with your family” and the treatment was, “You should read this with your kids”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate the results, but we did see a directional lift from the treatment. This tells us that the specificity from the treatment minimally helped increase engagement, but wasn't a significant enough difference to have a greater impact.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: LWF – Email Subject Line Test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will produce the best results for LWF?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/15/2021
Testing subject lines and the preheader. The results on opens and clicks were very similar, however the treatment had a higher conversion rate when it came to donations. While not above the 95% confidence level it does give us something to build upon.
experiments.keyLearnings: The subject line that was tested may have done a better job of attracting people that wanted to get the series as it framed the content as what a person would get out of the book when they read it.

experiments.title: How a relevant subject line affected housefile engagement for a cultivation email

rows.name: Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing “you” to add relevance in a subject line for a cultivation series to the housefile will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
We were noticing a recent decline in open rate for a weekly cultivation email series to the housefile so we wanted to test some subject line elements to see how we could improve how the audience was responding to different subject lines and increase engagement with the cultivation emails. We established a control—“An interesting interview”—using the element of mystery to invoke curiosity and a treatment—“Are you familiar with Mattress Mack?”—using the word you to create relevancy for the email recipients. We set up the A/B split test to see which of the two elements had better performance so we can continue to adjust subject lines for future emails in the series by using what works for this particular audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was set up in Hubspot to monitor results for the first 4 hours after the email test was sent to 50% of the total audience and then send the email with the most opens to the remainder of the file. Because of this, we did not have a large enough sample size to validate the experiment. The treatment saw a 6.3% decrease in traffic, but we did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence to draw any conclusions based on these results. Moving forward, we will want to run a similar test as a split to the full housefile rather than just half of it so we can validate results and find out how to best optimize the subject lines for this audience.

experiments.title: How a subject line test impacted open rates on a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating a new subject line for returning donors will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We developed a treatment that used more active rather than passive language. The control subject line asked the question “Are you ready?” while the treatment asked “Are you coming?” while the body copy remained the same for both versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see any valid results from the experiment and only reached 50.2% level of confidence so this concept would need to be re-tested in the future. Looking at the body copy for the treatment, we would want to also test aligning the body copy with the subject line to make sure the original question is addressed or expounded upon to help the message flow more congruently.

experiments.title: How a more humanized subject line affects open rate on an email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized subject line for a housefile audience will achieve an increase in open rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2021
In an effort to increase overall email engagement during a two-week high urgency annual campaign, we wanted to test various subject lines to measure open and click response. We wanted to test the effect of a more humanized subject line vs. a standard subject line in an email. The control subject line read “Membership question” and the treatment read “I hope you will be joining us.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a .69% increase in traffic on the treatment but this test did not validate because it resulted in a confidence level of 49.2%. This would require retesting and tested with a prospecting email instead of a housefile email in order to test a larger sample size. We also recommend testing a more humanized subject line again using a first name in the subject line instead of "you" if testing the housefile email again.
