experiments.title: How including a hot button issue in the subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will mentioning free speech in the subject line improve the open rate of the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2018 - 07/14/2018
The Leadership Institute works with college students to help defend their free speech on campus. They wanted to see if including hot button terms like “free speech” and “the one percent” in the subject line would affect open rates. Their hypothesis was that the subject line about free speech would generate more opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rates for each email were no more than .4% deviations away from 20%. This experiment was not conclusive. It’s likely that the hot button issues did not affect open rate as much due to the specified name of the school in the subject line. A further experiment without a specified name may render desired results to confidently conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?

rows.name: Fundraising Content You’ll Love… see what I did there…
Fundraising Content You’ll ❤️… see what I did there…

experiments.researchQuestion: Will an emoji in the subject line help increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2020 - 02/28/2020
People use emoji’s all the time and we get asked how they can or should be used in emails and subject lines. We didn’t have any good experiments available to justify our thinking that it isn’t a worthwhile test so decided to test it for ourselves. We were sending a digest email on Valentines day so tied in the subject line to ‘love’ for the control and for the treatment we used a heart emoji in its place.
experiments.keyLearnings: Short answer: nope, didn’t do anything. With a 25% level of confidence, the emoji didn’t seem to have any impact on the likelihood of a subscriber to open.

experiments.title: How changing the sender name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increases open rates for Daily Prayer
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2019
We’ve previously tested the sender name for other emails and found that using Fr. David increased open rates, but we wanted to see if it would do the same for our Daily Prayer that we send out every morning.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that using Fr. David did increase open rates, but not as much as our regular email did. We think because these recipients are so used to seeing the subject line Daily Prayer every morning that the sender name might not be as important to them.

experiments.title: How changing Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to just Fr. David increases open rates for Daily Prayer
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the name from Fr. David P. Uribe, OMI to Fr. David increase open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/31/2019
We’ve previously tested the sender name for other emails and found that using Fr. David increased open rates, but we wanted to see if it would do the same for our Daily Prayer that we send out every morning.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that using Fr. David did increase open rates, but not as much as our regular email did. We think because these recipients are so used to seeing the subject line Daily Prayer every morning that the sender name might not be as important to them.

experiments.title: How indicating the time commitment of an email impacts open rate

rows.name: Why the "village" can't replace parents (4 min read)

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the time commitment to the subject line increase open rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2018 - 11/10/2018
For the weekly cultivation email sent out by Care Net, we wanted to see if we could increase the email engagement by letting subscribers know the time commitment required to consume the topic. This is something often seen on blog posts but we wanted to see if it would have a positive impact in email. To do this, we added ” (4 min read)” to the end of the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a nearly identical open rate between the two versions. After further reflection, it is our hypothesis that the longer subject line may have resulted in the “(4 min read)” not being shown in the inbox. Having this aspect cut off would have resulted in the two variants being identical.

experiments.title: How more mystery in a subject line affects click-rate

rows.name: The webinar recording is ready for you

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line with more mystery lead to more opens and clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2019 - 07/22/2019
In this webinar follow-up email, we had been using the same kind of subject line for a while. While the sample size is low and hard to validate on, we wanted to put a hypothesis to the test that a subject line with more mystery would lead to more opens and clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: As we assumed, our sample size was not large enough to validate the result, despite seeing an 11% increase in clicks from the treatment version. A more radical test will be required in order to see a statistically confidence result.

experiments.title: How switching the email subject line to place the words gift and prayer first increases opens
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

rows.name: Enroll a Loved One Today - Share the Gift of Prayer
Share the Gift of Prayer - Enroll a Loved One Today

experiments.researchQuestion: The question that we we’re wanting to answer is if this worked for every email. Would people be more willing to click if they noticed gift and prayer first in the subject line or would they still click on the email if that was towards the end of the subject line.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2019
We tested this through Mailchimp. We’ve noticed in previous tests and throughout our website analytics that most of the donors respond more when they see the words gift or prayer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key takeaway is that the results were almost completely identical. This could be because we also had words like Loved Ones at the beginning of the email that also usually generates a lot of interest with our audience. Moving forward when running a test like this we will have to make sure there is no words that could make an impact emotionally like Loved Ones is.

experiments.title: How the landing page design can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another. Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as "approaching validity." This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How a “forwarded” email impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a forward email impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2019
Focus on the Family ran a year end campaign in 2018. They hypothesized that sending an email that looked like a “FWD” would increase engagement and conversion. They decided to test this against an email with a subject line that didn’t have “FWD” in it.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment is not valid because the level of confidence is only 51.2%. Having a forwarded email has no impact on conversion. The reason for this is because motivation is so high of the year. In the context of a year end campaign, a forwarded email has no impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How noting the specific content offer type in the subject line affects conversion

rows.name: A full year’s worth of new fundraising ideas to test
A full year’s worth of new fundraising ideas to test [WEBINAR]

experiments.researchQuestion: Will noting that the email is about a webinar in the subject line increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/25/2019
In this experiment, we wondered if clarifying the type of content that the email was about in the subject line would lead to greater conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: This change had no affect on conversion. It’s possible that it may have had a more significant effect if placed at the beginning of the subject line, rather than the end. The end of the subject line could tend to get cut off on many devices and email platforms.

experiments.title: How a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius affected opens

rows.name: The roots of Jane Austen's genius

experiments.researchQuestion: Will a subject line test of a famous author’s first novel vs the root of their creative genius increase opens?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2018 - 04/20/2018
This experiment was conducted to see if a change in subject line between Jane Austen’s first novel and the root of her creative genius had any effect on open rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rate for the control was 14% and the open rate for the treatment was 14.1%. This means there was no significant difference in responses to the subject lines. For future experiments, we will need to test more radical changes to the subject line.
